Good to see you here Daniel. Be sure to share your progress on your project as it evolves!

That is a very strange way to program. The mouse box has a bunch of different connection points, and the connection points have very specific meaning. This requires a tremendous memory to use, and I would be unable to use this product ever.

I think that it is somehow problematic. By definition you need to return much more than the VC gave you on the profit route. If the other route is “only” to apply for money by other non profits that would be a problem.

I think that it is somehow problematic. By definition you need to return much more than the VC gave you on the profit route. If the other route is “only” to apply for money by other non profits that would be a problem.

I know you still think that your main venue is in computer science. But technology does not exist in a vacuum, as shown by the two options above. So the question basically is: how do You create something that is of value to others? While still incorporating the risk/possibility of failure?

In my opinion you can’t dance around the matter of fact that you need trust. You will need people to trust you.
In my opinion you can’t dance around the matter of fact that you need trust. You will need people to trust you.

A side rant here on Alan’s /VPRIs STEPs project. As I comprehend it the project got public funding (NSF) but how was the process of the project conducted? Papers were written and talks were held and some code was written (no hardware as far as I remember)

A side rant here on Alan’s /VPRIs STEPs project. As I comprehend it the project got public funding (NSF) but how was the process of the project conducted? Papers were written and talks were held and some code was written (no hardware as far as I remember)
So the project still had powerful ideas in it, as the eager interest in the gezira/Nile projects on github shows
So the project still had powerful ideas in it, as the eager interest in the gezira/Nile projects on github shows
But guess what the software is not available in a coherent format which is usable by the public.
But guess what the software is not available in a coherent format which is usable by the public.
So instead of thinking in terms of papers and code I challenge you <@U5TCAFTD3> to go beyond that
So instead of thinking in terms of papers and code I challenge you <@U5TCAFTD3> to go beyond that

It is like Juan Benet said in that ycombinator interview. Yes you need long term projects which also means longer term funding to really achieve something. But I fundamentally believe you need also a different way to connect to the public/people

It is like Juan Benet said in that ycombinator interview. Yes you need long term projects which also means longer term funding to really achieve something. But I fundamentally believe you need also a different way to connect to the public/people

To be a bit more concrete: a physical space where people can meet other people and talk about the idea of the project. The lab of Bret victor or the <https://www.recurse.com/> center might be such places.

attachments.title: The Recurse Center
attachments.text: The Recurse Center is a self-directed, community-driven educational retreat for programmers in New York City.

text: But you really do need a place where people can engage with each other. Of course research needs different conditions
But you really do need a place where people can engage with each other. Of course research needs different conditions
But my point is you need both: research _and_ open dialog
But my point is you need both: research _and_ open dialog
Ideally you would need such Center on each continent for the start. Maker /fab labs could be a good starting point.

Just don’t get caught in the Silicon Valley bubble. Because it really is a bubble. And even worse see Bret thoughts dilemma about how his creation may not be ‘captured’ . It starts with trust it starts with people. This slack was a start, but a start it is.

The scariest thing for programmers:

Legacy code
No documentation
Single letter variable names

I was looking at the video and thinking that I would have a big "empty canvas problem" when starting with an idea of what I want but not sure how to do it, even as a programmer. It's reall cool to see someone who knows it use it, but that may be the case for any programmer using any tool :slightly_smiling_face:

<!everyone> quick notice: please treat this Slack as a public, archived record. It is currently available publicly here[1] and may be in other places in the future. This Slack has always been open to the public, so I expect this to come as no surprise, but <@UFEQUBNNT> brought to my attention that we should be super explicit about this, and thus this message. If you have private things to say, please don't say them in public Slack channels. Thanks!

Hot take: Pumpkin carving is imperative, not declarative. My suggestion is definitionally simple, but procedurally difficult to carve. Very difficult.

More serious suggestion: `( )`

Hey Parris, some quick first impressions: I find this line to be kind of confusing: "Old document creators print to paper. This one publishes to phone.". People are already pretty comfortable with docs being online, so not sure the paper reference works.

Maybe something more like "Don't just write a doc, tell a story" (prob not good either, but just a thought).

I like the second paragraph except for "documents should be the next best thing to a conversation.". Don't really know what that means.

Animated GIFs are a nice touch. This looks really slick and I can totally get why someone might use this instead of a Google Doc if they're trying to present or persuade.

I do like the comparison to docs (or maybe slideshows?) over calling it a "simple app builder" or "micro-experience creator". Lots of people make docs/slideshows, probably no one is thinking "I want to make a tiny app".

The Y combinator. `Y f = f (Y f)` or `Y = λf.(λx.(f (x x)) λx.(f (x x)))`. Scary because: (1) can be used to demonstrate that untyped lambda calculus is unsound as a deductive system; (2) causes an infinite loop when applied to most functions; (3) single letter variable names.

Just posted a handy color picker utility web app written in beads that lets you copy a color name or Hex value to the clipboard. It sorts the colors nicely, and resizes to a very wide range of window sizes. I believe that it is within 20% of the theoretical minimum program word count to achieve this task. 0 external dependencies, and only about a dozen functions total, how much simpler can it get? Anyway please check out the code at <https://github.com/magicmouse/beads-examples/tree/master/Example%20-%20Color%20Picker> or just use the utility at <http://beadslang.com/apps/color_picker/picker.html>

Hi everybody! I’m yet another child of HyperCard hoping to recapture the dream of the ’90s. I’m currently working with <@UD3AK9JRF> on an end-user thing; we’re experimenting with building it using a simple Datalog-like model.
Divergently, I’ve spent a lot of time in academia building ecological and evolutionary models, all the while dreaming about more natural ways for scientists to work with computers. (And doing basically nothing about it.)
Less concretely, I’m generally hopeful for a future where many more people view computers as infinitely flexible instruments of creative expression rather than devices limited by what professional software developers are willing to provide.
I’m also interested in how power structures in society have affected the way we use computers—and what we can do about it.
I’m here for the conversations!
<@UE3KC0VK6> Congrats! What are you working on there? (Assuming that isn't too confidential a topic.)

I used the fabulous TekTronix 4014 storage terminal screen back in 1977. It had an incredible 4000 x 3000 pixels, easily 10x what other devices had at the time. To erase the screen you had to press a zap button. The image was rock solid though, as it was etched in slow phosphors, so no flicker at all... It came with two clumsy thumbwheels for XY input, before they had trackballs and mice. Even more astonishing, it had a thermal printer that printed the screen for you in grayscale in a few seconds. Developed for the military, it was such great gear, and Tektronix was and probably still is a great company with excellent products.

To erase the screen you had to press a zap button. The image was rock solid though, as it was etched in slow phosphors, so no flicker at all... It came with two clumsy thumbwheels for XY input, before they had trackballs and mice. Even more astonishing, it had a thermal printer that printed the screen for you in grayscale in a few seconds. Developed for the military, it was such great gear, and Tektronix was and probably still is a great company with excellent products.

It is true that the early supporters of computers were the defense dept., banks, and insurance companies. But now with a supercomputer in a billion pockets, we are really only limited by our ability to program. There is however a conspiracy among the tech giants to keep things super complex and under their control, so that no upstart company can crack their monopolies. Complexity is the main weapon used to dominate, and i think you will find many revolutionaries lurking in this slack group, dedicated to toppling the status quo. As <@U6KQ2S410> pointed out the other day, we were promised bicycles for the mind but were given aircraft carriers instead!

Steve Jobs gets a tour of Xerox PARC and is shown Alan Kay's vision of personal dynamic media, embodied by Smalltalk and the Alto (the interrim Dynabook). Jobs takes that vision and gradually perverts it into a system of digital slavery. In Jobs' vision, computers are appliances that are not end user programmable. You can only install Apps, which are only available from the walled garden of the App Store.
